notes: The term has reference to many aspects of the sacred, all of which are directly connected to its root meaning, the separation of the sacred from the profane. Specifically, qds has reference to: 1. God, his name, and his divine actions (Exodus 15:11; Leviticus 20:3). 2. The set-apartness and sanctity of holy places connected with Deity, such as the Tabernacle (Exodus 38:24; 40:9), the Jerusalem temple (2 Chronicles 29:5), the Ezekielian temple (Ezekiel 42:14), the city of Jerusalem (Isaiah 48:2), and the land of Israel (Zechariah 2:12). 3. Things directly associated with sacred places, such as the temple furniture (Exodus 30:29; 2 Chronicles 35:3), the altar (Exodus 29:37; Deuteronomy 9:24), anointing oil (Exodus 30:25), incense (Exodus 30:35), priestly vestments (Leviticus 16:4), and the bread of the presence (1 Samuel 21:5). 4. Persons directly associated with sacred places, such as the priests (Leviticus 21:6) and the people of Israel (Jeremiah 2:3; Psalms 114:2). 5. Holy days and festivals (Isaiah 58:13; Exodus 35:2). (Donald W. Parry, "Ritual Anointing with Olive Oil in Ancient Israelite Religion," The Allegory of the Olive Tree, 262.)

Inscribed on each temple are the words "Holiness to the Lord." That statement designates both the temple and its purposes as holy. Those who enter the temple are also to bear the attribute of holiness. It may be easier to ascribe holiness to a building than it is to a people. We can acquire holiness only by enduring and persistent personal effort. Through the ages, servants of the Lord have warned against unholiness. Jacob, brother of Nephi, wrote, "I would speak unto you of holiness; but as ye are not holy, and ye look upon me as a teacher, [I] must … teach you the consequences of sin." (Russell M. Nelson, Ensign, May 2001, 32)

It is necessary to discuss the meaning of the Hebrew root qds (qadash), which in English is translated "to sanctify." In the Hebrew Bible the principal root from which the English word temple originates is also qds, which has the basic meaning of "separation" or "withdrawal" of sacred entities from profane things.

quotes.text: We can acquire holiness only by enduring and persistent personal effort. Through the ages, servants of the Lord have warned against unholiness. Jacob, brother of Nephi, wrote, "I would speak unto you of holiness; but as ye are not holy, and ye look upon me as a teacher, [I] must … teach you the consequences of sin."

notes: In other words, the only way that God can ultimately get HIS PEOPLE to become HIS PEOPLE is to separate them from the rest of mankind. That is another way the wilderness portrays the millennium. They are separated, and the only thing they have to learn is HIS LAW. That is where we also will LEARN TO LIVE BY EVERY WORD THAT PROCEEDETH FORTH FROM THE MOUTH OF GOD. That is what the millennium is. The Lord teaches at different levels. He took them from Egypt into the wilderness to teach them to be separate and holy; the next model is when the real Deliverer comes to take us from the real Egypt into the real wilderness, the Millennium, to separate us from the real telestial world. So at the real millennium, the righteous will be separated from the wicked when the Lord brings his laws. Under todays conditions, as good as we try to be, and try to be good, until there is an entire separation, and we have only God's laws, and we can concentrate on one thing only...the separation will help us learn to be holy. God can't really do that until he separates us out from the world. That is what God did with the city of Enoch. He had to separate them from the world and then took them off to a Terrestrial state. We are in the process of learning about God and his ways, and when the world is wicked enough, and we have done all we can, then the separation will come. Even in the church, the virgins referred to are the wise and the foolish virgins, will all be together, and the separation will divide them also. See what you can learn from the law of Moses by having it point to the big plan; Every whit of the law points to the great and last sacrifice of Christ. That will free us from the bondage of this world. Now Leviticus 26 - very important chapter; God explains his attitude toward those who keep the covenant.1. You make no idols; 2. You keep the sabbath day holy and reverence the temple; 3. Ye walk in my statutes, and keep my commandments, here is what I will do. 4. Rain in due season, land will yield, trees give fruit; 5. plenty to eat; 6. Peace in the land, security. 7. Chase enemies, and they will fall (Robert Norman, 1989 Old Testament Class Notes)
